coredata.dc:title: Performance and mechanism of ZIF-67 composite carbon dots for activation of peroxymonosulfate for Bisphenol A degradation

coredata.dc:description: The NCDs/ZIF-67 catalyst was synthesized to activate peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and degrade bisphenol A (BPA) by simple modification of ZIF-67, a stable Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) with good catalytic properties and room-temperature crystallization, using carbon dot materials (CDs), which are available from a wide range of sources and inexpensive. The doping amount of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) in NCDs/ZIF-67 was optimized, and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 with the optimal catalytic performance was obtained. After reaction for 30 min, the removal of BPA reached 94.87 %, which was 52.61 % higher than that of ZIF-67. The morphology, crystal structure, surface elements valence and functional groups of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were obtained through characterization, and the successful synthesis of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was confirmed. The effects of reaction conditions and coexisting substances on the degradation of BPA were investigated. Among them, NCDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized under mild conditions, with the advantages of environmental protection, economy and stability. Repeated degradation experiment and Characterization results before and after the reaction of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 indicated that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 had good reuse performance. The results of tests illustrated the degradation of BPA by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS involved both free radical interactions (SO4 ·−、·OH、O2 ·−) and non-radical pathways (1O2 generation and electron transfer), in which the non-radical interactions played a major role. To detect BPA degradation intermediates, three possible degradation pathways of BPA were proposed. The mechanism of enhanced catalytic performance of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 compared with ZIF-67 was studied. The doping of NCDs did not significantly improve the adsorption performance of the material, but increased the catalytic active sites of the composite, improved the hydrophilicity of the composites, and reduced the surface impedance and charge transfer impedance of the materials during the catalytic reaction. NCDs doping minimally enhanced the adsorption performance of the materials, but improved the hydrophilicity of ZIF-67 and increased the active sites for the catalytic degradation of pollutants; at the same time, it could promote the electron transfer on the surface of the composite materials in the catalytic reaction, which lowered the surface impedance and charge transfer impedance of the materials in the process of the catalytic reaction and enhanced the catalytic performance. In this study, an efficient heterogeneous catalyst was developed for the efficient degradation of the endocrine disruptor BPA by improving the non-radical pathway and catalytic stability. This study aims to provide a green and reusable solution for the treatment of BPA contamination in water bodies.

head.ce:title: Performance and mechanism of ZIF-67 composite carbon dots for activation of peroxymonosulfate for Bisphenol A degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of the Three Gorges Reservoir Region's Eco-Environment (Ministry of Education), Chongqing University, Chongqing 400045, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Three Gorges Reservoir Region's Eco-Environment (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of the Three Gorges Reservoir Region'’s Eco-Environment (Ministry of Education), Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400045, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The NCDs/ZIF-67 catalyst was synthesized to activate peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and degrade bisphenol A (BPA) by simple modification of ZIF-67, a stable Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) with good catalytic properties and room-temperature crystallization, using carbon dot materials (CDs), which are available from a wide range of sources and inexpensive. The doping amount of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) in NCDs/ZIF-67 was optimized, and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 with the optimal catalytic performance was obtained. After reaction for 30 min, the removal of BPA reached 94.87 %, which was 52.61 % higher than that of ZIF-67. The morphology, crystal structure, surface elements valence and functional groups of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were obtained through characterization, and the successful synthesis of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was confirmed. The effects of reaction conditions and coexisting substances on the degradation of BPA were investigated. Among them, NCDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized under mild conditions, with the advantages of environmental protection, economy and stability.

Repeated degradation experiment and Characterization results before and after the reaction of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 indicated that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 had good reuse performance. The results of tests illustrated the degradation of BPA by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS involved both free radical interactions (SO4 ·−、·OH、O2 ·−) and non-radical pathways (1O2 generation and electron transfer), in which the non-radical interactions played a major role. To detect BPA degradation intermediates, three possible degradation pathways of BPA were proposed. The mechanism of enhanced catalytic performance of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 compared with ZIF-67 was studied. The doping of NCDs did not significantly improve the adsorption performance of the material, but increased the catalytic active sites of the composite, improved the hydrophilicity of the composites, and reduced the surface impedance and charge transfer impedance of the materials during the catalytic reaction. NCDs doping minimally enhanced the adsorption performance of the materials, but improved the hydrophilicity of ZIF-67 and increased the active sites for the catalytic degradation of pollutants; at the same time, it could promote the electron transfer on the surface of the composite materials in the catalytic reaction, which lowered the surface impedance and charge transfer impedance of the materials in the process of the catalytic reaction and enhanced the catalytic performance.

In this study, an efficient heterogeneous catalyst was developed for the efficient degradation of the endocrine disruptor BPA by improving the non-radical pathway and catalytic stability. This study aims to provide a green and reusable solution for the treatment of BPA contamination in water bodies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A has been synthesized in large quantities and is widely used in plastics [1], polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins, and its polymers are used in large quantities in the manufacture of industrial products and the manufacture of suntan lotions, nail polishes, body washes, etc., but improper disposal of BPA compounds has made BPA frequently detected in water bodies [2,3]. As a typical environmental endocrine disruptor, BPA exerts its biological effects through the estrogen receptor-mediated pathway and tends to accumulate in aquatic organisms [4]. Epidemiological studies have confirmed that chronic exposure to BPA is associated with reproductive disorders, neurodevelopmental abnormalities, and metabolic syndrome [5], including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and several types of cancer. Despite the adjustment of TDI from 50 μg/kg-d to 4 μg/kg-d by the European Union in 2012 and a ban on BPA in baby products in 11 countries, including Canada, annual global production of BPA has increased rapidly from 5.2 million tonnes in 2008 to 8.6 million tonnes (2025 estimate) [6,7]. Current environmental monitoring data show that BPA is widely detected in wastewater treatment plant effluents (0.15–21.3 μg/L) [8,9], surface water sediments (0.8–208 μg/kg dw) [10], and urine samples from >90 % of the population [11], suggesting that exposure pathways are diverse and in need of control. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) has set the oral reference dose of BPA as the total allowable concentration (TAC) of BPA in drinking water at 100 μg/L [12]; China has listed BPA as a reference indicator for domestic drinking water quality with a limit of 0.01 mg/L. However, many reports have detected BPA concentrations higher than this in aqueous environments suggesting the diversity of exposure pathways and the need for control. Therefore, the removal of BPA from the aqueous environment is important for environmental protection and biosafety.

BPA has endocrine disrupting properties, and the harm to the environment and human health could not be ignored [13]. It is very important to remove BPA from aqueous environment. Current BPA removal technologies include mainly adsorption [14,15], biodegradation [16,17], membrane separation [18,19], and advanced oxidation [20,21]. Physical methods (adsorption/membrane separation) are simple to operate but do not disrupt the structure of BPA, and there is a risk of contaminant enrichment and transfer or high-pressure operating conditions; biological degradation is inefficient and has a long cycle time. In contrast, advanced oxidation technology, which can effectively degrade BPA into non-toxic small molecule compounds, has a significant advantage in achieving rapid mineralization of BPA through the generation of hydroxyl (·OH) and sulfate (SO₄·−) radicals. In particular, the peroxomonosulphate (PMS) and peroxodisulphate (PDS) systems, which combine high oxidation potential, a wide pH range, and higher selective oxidation and oxidation efficiency, are more effective in removing emerging pollutants [22]. Thanks to the asymmetric structure, the advanced oxidation technology based on active PMS has the advantages of high oxidation capacity and wide application range in the process of BPA removal [23].PMS can be activated by ultraviolet, light, heat, ultrasound, transition metal ions and their metal oxides and carbon materials [24].

MOFs are crystal materials constructed from the coordination of metal ions or clusters with organic linkers, forming periodic network structures. Among the methods for PMS activation, MOFs have the advantages of large specific surface area, good catalytic performance and stability, and easy functionalization, and their tunable pore structure and customizable catalytic sites can enhance the generation of active species, which have very potential as non-homogeneous phase catalysts for PMS activation for BPA degradation, making them a hot spot in current research and synthesis. Depending on the constituent building units, MOFs can be categorized into various series: Isoreticular Metal Organic Frameworks (IRMOFs), Porous Coordination Networks (PCNs), Coordination Pillar Layered series (CPL), UiO series (UiO), Materials Institute Lavoisier (MIL), and Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks (ZIFs) [25]. Among these, ZIFs exhibit superior thermal and chemical stability compared to other MOFs. Additionally, ZIFs can be synthesized under mild conditions at room temperature, which lays the foundation for their application as catalysts to activate PMS for the treatment of pollutants in complex aqueous environments.

ZIFs are composed of divalent transition metal centers (primarily Zn2+and Co2+) and imidazolate-based linkers. The ZIFs series have been reported for the synthesis and activation of PMS to degrade pollutants include: ZIF-2 [26], ZIF-8 [27], ZIF-9, ZIF-12 [28], ZIF-67 [29], etc. The zeolitic imidazole frameworks ZIF-67, which is composed of cobalt ion and 2-methylimidazole [30], has good catalytic performance and stability [31], and is promising as a catalyst for the degradation of BPA by activating PMS. However, ZIF-67 still has shortcomings such as insufficient catalytic sites and poor electron transfer efficiency in catalytic reaction. In addition, the synthetic ZIF-67 is hydrophobic [32], resulting in fewer contact sites with pollutants when reacting in water [33]. Therefore, it is necessary to functionalize and modify ZIF-67 to enhance the catalytic performance. The ZIF-67 modification can significantly improve the catalytic performance and structural stability through the composite synergy of metal nanoparticles [34], metal oxides [35,36], MOF materials [37], and carbon materials [38]. However, the traditional modified materials are susceptible to ligand oxidation and irreversible metal oxidation in aqueous advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) environments, which leads to insufficient long-term stability; and pyrolytically derived metal oxides/carbon catalysts, although can alleviate the hydrolysis problem [39–41], are faced with high energy consumption. In recent years, green synthesis strategies have focused on carbon-based composite modification, using carbon fibers, graphene, and other materials to inhibit metal dissolution, among which CDs can show unique advantages in optimizing the degradation efficiency of pollutants in the ZIF-67/PMS system due to the modulation of the surface functional groups and the electron transfer characteristics, which provides a new path to break the bottleneck of the industrialization and application of MOFs materials.

CDs are discrete, quasi-spherical carbon particles with sizes <10 nm [42], with abundant surface functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, etc., which give them excellent water solubility and easy modification properties [43]. Doping of CDs can improve the hydrophilicity of ZIF-67, and increase the contact sites and active sites for catalytic degradation of pollutants. Meanwhile, CDs has excellent electron transfer properties [44] and can be used as a medium to promote electron transfer on the surface of composites, thus enhancing the catalytic performance. In addition, the properties of CDs can be further improved by element doping [45,46]. Nitrogen atoms are often doped into CDs due to their similar size to carbon atoms and more electronegativity than carbon atoms.

In this study, NCDs were designed to modify ZIF-67, and the catalyst NCDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized to activate PMS to degrade BPA. The synthesis of NCDs/ZIF-67 was optimized to enhance BPA removal. The optimized NCDs/ZIF-67 was characterized to obtain physicochemical related information. In addition, the performance and the degradation mechanism of BPA degradation by NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS were studied. The mechanism of enhanced catalytic performance of NCDs/ZIF-67 was also explored.

In this study, ZIF-67 was surface-modified with CDs to establish the non-radical pathway simultaneously by enhancing hydrophilicity and electron conductivity. Based on the modified NCDs/ZIF-67 catalysts, firstly, the physicochemical properties of the materials were optimally characterized and clarified to improve the removal rate of BPA; secondly, the degradation efficacy and degradation mechanism of the PMS-activated system for BPA were investigated; then, the mechanism of enhancing the catalytic performance was analyzed; and ultimately, a green and sustainable method of removing pollutant BPA was realized. This study provides theoretical support for the engineering application of MOFs-based catalysts in the field of advanced oxidation.
The specifics regarding chemical reagents in the experiment and other relevant details can be found in the Text S1.

The preparation of NCDs was slightly adjusted according to the previous literature [47]. Firstly, 1.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g acrylamide were dissolved into 10 mL formamide. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stain less steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The obtained product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to obtain a carbon dot solution. After that, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and the filtrate was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The preparation steps of NCDs/ZIF-67 are as follows: 1.3136 g 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 30 mL methanol, then the designed amount of NCDs was added to the above solution, and solution A was formed by ultrasonic stirring at 25 kHz for 5 min. Then, 1.1641 g Co(NO3)2•6H2O was dissolved in 30 mL methanol to form solution B. Solution B was slowly poured into solution A at room temperature and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 400 rpm for 24 h. The mixture was allowed to rest for 12 h, then washed with methanol using a high-speed centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the process was repeated three times. Finally, NCDs/ZIF-67 was obtained by drying at 60 °C for 24 h in a vacuum drying oven. The composites synthesized by adding 10, 20, 50, 100, 300, and 500 μL NCDs during the synthesis process of NCDs/ZIF-67 were named 10NCDs/ZIF-67, 20NCDs/ZIF-67, 50NCDs/ZIF-67, 100NCDs/ZIF-67, 300NCDs/ZIF-67 and 500NCDs/ZIF-67, respectively. Under the same conditions, ZIF-67 was prepared without adding NCDs.

The steps of BPA degradation experiment were as follows: the prepared 5 mg of catalyst was added into the 100 mL of 20 mg/L BPA solution, at the same time, PMS was added into the pollutant solution to achieve the concentration of 0.4 mmol/L in the reaction system. The mixture was stirred magnetically at the speed of 500 rpm to disperse the catalyst uniformly. During the reaction, samples were taken at a certain interval. A pipette was used to transfer 1 mL of the water sample into a centrifuge tube containing 1 mL methanol, and the mixture was mixed evenly. Then 1 mL of the mixture was aspirated by a syringe and filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane, and the concentration of residual BPA was determined. By comparing the removal and reaction rate of BPA with different NCDs doped catalysts under the same reaction conditions, NCDs/ZIF-67 with the optimal doping amount of CDs was obtained. The conditions for BPA detection were as follows: mobile phase methanol to water = 70 ∶ 30 (V ∶ V), flow rate 1.0 mL/min, injection volume of 20 μL, determination wavelength of 276 nm, and column temperature of 35 °C. The BPA mineralization experiments were conducted as follows: A 100 mL solution containing 10 mg/L BPA was treated with 5 mg of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 catalyst. PMS was subsequently introduced to achieve a final concentration of 0.4 mmol/L in the reaction system. The suspension was magnetically stirred at 500 rpm to ensure homogeneous dispersion of the catalyst. Aliquots (5 mL) were collected at predetermined time intervals (0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, and 90 min) and immediately quenched with 2 mL of 0.5 mol/L Na2S2O3 solution. After dilution to 25 mL with deionized water, 20 mL of the mixture was syringe-filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane. The resulting filtrate was subsequently analyzed for total organic carbon (TOC) concentration to evaluate mineralization efficiency.

The concentration of BPA was determined by Agilent 1260 high performance liquid chromatography. The conditions for BPA detection were as follows: mobile phase methanol to water = 70: 30 (V ∶ V), flow rate 1.0 mL/min, injection volume 20 μL, wavelength 276 nm, column temperature 35 °C. Liquid chromatography (Agilent 1290uplc) coupled with mass spectrometry (Agilent qtof6550) was used to detect the intermediates of BPA in the degradation process. The chromatographic column was waters BEH C18 (2.1 × 100 mm, 1.7 μm). The specific detection conditions were as follows: mobile phase A was 0.1 % formic acid aqueous solution; mobile phase B was acetonitrile solution, and the gradient settings of the mobile phase were as shown in Table S1; the flow rate was 0.3 mL/min; the injection volume was 5 uL; the scanning range of the mass spectrometry was from 50 to 800 m/z; the temperature of the sheath gas was 350 °C, and the flow rate was 12 mL/min, using electrospray ion source ESI− mode, voltage 3200 V. The active species in the system were detected by Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, EMXplus-6/1, Bruker), SO4 •− and •OH were captured in ultrapure water solvent and O2 •− in methanol solvent with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-n-oxide (DMPO) as trapping agent. 1O2 was captured in ultrapure aqueous solvent using 2,2,6, 6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP) as trapping agent. The concentration of residual PMS was detected by iodide spectrophotometry [48,49]. The method was as follows: the iodide solution was prepared by adding potassium iodide to 0.05 mol/L sodium bicarbonate solution, making the concentration of potassium iodide in the mixture was 0.5 mol/L. Then, 0.1 mL of the sample filtrate was added to 4.9 mL of the above-prepared iodide solution, and the reaction was carried out for 20 min. The concentration of PMS in the filtrate was calculated by measuring the absorbance at 352 nm by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Spectroquant Pharo 300, Merck KGaA, Germany). More analytical and characterization methods were provided in the Text S2. BPA mineralization experiments were carried out using a Shimadzu TOC-LCPH total organic carbon analyser to determine the TOC concentration of the filtrate.

The amount of NCDs in NCDs/ZIF-67 was optimized to obtain optimal catalytic performance, the results are shown in Fig. 1 . When only PMS was present in the reaction system, the BPA concentration decreased by only 4.54 % for 30 min, indicating that PMS was not effectively activated for BPA degradation. When ZIF-67 was added, the removal of BPA was significantly increased to 42.27 % and the reaction rate was 0.0194 min−1.

As the doping amount of NCDs increased from 10 to 100 μL, the removal of BPA by NCDs/ZIF-67 was significantly enhanced. After reaction for 30 min, the removal of BPA by 10NCDs/ZIF-67, 20NCDs/ZIF-67, 50NCDs/ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were 58.39 %, 77.25 %, 92.33 % and 94.87 %, respectively. And the reaction rate was increased by 0.58, 1.68, 3.50 and 4.47 times, respectively. However, as the doping amount of NCDs continued to increase to 300 and 500 μL, the removal of BPA decreased. After reaction for 30 min, the BPA removal by 300NCDs/ZIF-67 and 500NCDs/ZIF-67 were 90.83 % and 67.58 %, respectively, but still superior to the original ZIF-67. The BPA removal and reaction rate by NCDs/ZIF-67 increased firstly and then decreased with the increase of NCDs doping, and reached the optimum at 100 μL. When the doping amount of NCDs in the composite is small, NCDs can improve the catalytic performance by providing more reaction sites and contact sites, and promoting electron transfer in the catalytic reaction. However, excessive doping of NCDs may cover the main catalytic sites on the surface of ZIF-67, resulting in reduced catalytic performance and weakened electron transfer of NCDs.

The morphology and structural features of the catalysts were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Both ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed typical rhombic dodecahedral morphology, with disordered arrangement of the particles, and irregular pores generated by the stacking of the materials (Fig. 2 ). The crystal structure of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed sharp edges and corners, the surface was smooth, with a relatively uniform size distribution of about 1.0 to 1.5 μm. In contrast, the ZIF-67 particle structure has rounded edges, rougher surface, and a larger difference in particle size, which is between 0.5 and 1.0 μm. The doping of NCDs did not significantly change the morphology and structure of ZIF-67, but resulted in better crystallization, an increase in the grain size, and a more homogeneous and regular structure in terms of size and morphology, which might enhance the catalytic properties of the composites.

The morphology and structure of the synthesized catalytic material 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were further observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the results are shown in Fig. S1. The material 100NCDs/ZIF-67 shows a regular rhombic polyhedral structure with disordered arrangement between particles and a relatively uniform size distribution, which is consistent with its SEM image. In addition, the material 100NCDs/ZIF-67 has no obvious impurities inside and around the particles and has a regular profile, indicating that the material is well crystallized. At high resolution, the lattice stripes on the surface of the material 100NCDs/ZIF-67 can be observed, and this spacing was measured using Digital Micrograph software, and the result was 0.226 nm. At high resolution, the material NCDs are not clearly shown in the image, and it is difficult to confirm the successful doping of NCDs in ZIF-67 by TEM images. or the existence morphology of NCDs in ZIF-67, which still needs to be determined by using further characterization based on the nature of NCDs.

Catalysts ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were characterized by XRD to analyze their crystal structures, the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The XRD patterns of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 are similar, with strong diffraction peaks appearing at 10.36°, 12.70°, 18.00°, and 26.63°, which are attributed to the (002), (112), (222), and (134) crystalline planes of ZIF-67, respectively. In addition, diffraction peaks with relatively low intensities also appeared at 14.68°, 16.43°, 22.10°, 24.46°, 25.57°, 29.61°, 30.54°, and 32.30°, corresponding to the (022), (013), (114), (233), (224), (044), (344), and (235) crystal planes. Combined with SEM images, it could be inferred that ZIF-67 was successfully synthesized and the doping of NCDs in ZIF-67 did not change its crystal structure. The diffraction peaks of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were sharp, indicating that the materials were well crystallized. The crystallinity of the materials was further calculated using jade software. The crystallinity of ZIF-67 was about 93.74 %, and the crystallinity of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was nearly 100 %, which again indicated that the doping of NCDs in ZIF-67 facilitated the crystallization of the materials. This was consistent with the SEM observation results. Compared with ZIF-67, no new diffraction peaks appear in the XRD pattern of 100NCDs/ZIF-67, illustrating that NCDs might exist in an amorphous form in 100NCDs/ZIF-67.

It was difficult to confirm the presence of NCDs in 100NCDs/ZIF-67 through SEM images and XRD patterns which might be due to the small size of NCDs or the morphology and content of NCDs doped into ZIF-67. In order to confirm that NCDs were successfully doped into ZIF-67 to form a composite material, the fluorescence spectra of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were characterized by using the photoluminescence properties of NCDs, and the fluorescence emission patterns of the two catalysts were compared from 350 nm to 700 nm under the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Under 325 nm excitation, both ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 exhibited fluorescence peaks around 425 nm (Fig. S2), which might be generated by ZIF-67 material. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of ZIF-67 gradually decreased until 700 nm. While 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed a lower intensity fluorescence peak near 650 nm, which was generated by the photoluminescence characteristics of NCDs. Thus, it could be confirmed that NCDs were successfully doped into 100NCDs/ZIF-67.

In order to analyze the elements and valence states on the catalysts surface, XPS characterization was performed. Catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed four characteristic peaks at 781.8, 531.9, 399.2 and 284.9 eV, which were attributed to Co 2p, O 1 s, N 1 s and C1s, respectively (Fig. S3). The fine spectra of the above elements were further tested, and sub-peak fitting results were shown in Fig. S4. The C 1 s fine spectrum showed four characteristic peaks at 288.2, 286.8, 286.2 and 284.8 eV, corresponding to O–C=O, CN, O–C–O and CC bonds, respectively [50]. In the fine spectrum of O 1 s, the characteristic peaks of O–C=O and CoO appeared at 533.5 and 531.7 eV. In the Co 2p fine spectrum, the peaks at the binding energies of 796.6 and 781.0 eV were attributed to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2, respectively. While the peaks at 802.7 and 786.9 eV were satellite peaks of Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2, respectively [51]. In addition, the N 1 s fine spectrum displayed characteristic peaks of pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen at 400.3 and 398.9 eV, respectively [52]. Pyridine nitrogen was the main nitrogen type.

The functional groups of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were analyzed by FTIR. Most absorption peaks appeared simultaneously at the same wavenumber for both materials (Fig. S5). The absorption peak at 3432 cm−1 was due to the OH stretching vibration of water adsorbed on the materials, while the absorption peaks at 3134 and 2925 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibration of the CH sp2 bond of the aliphatic hydrocarbon chain and the stretching vibration of the CH sp3 bond of the aromatic ring, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1582 and 756 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration [53] and the out-of-plane vibration [54] of CN, respectively. The absorption peak at 1453 cm−1 belonged to the antisymmetric bending vibration of –CH3, and the absorption peak at 1418 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of –C=C [55]. The absorption peaks at 1303 and 693 cm−1 were due to the bending vibration of 2-methylimidazole ring [56]; the absorption peaks at 1170 and 1141 cm−1 belonged to the CN stretching vibration peaks; the absorption peak at 991 cm−1 corresponded to the CN bending vibration peak [57]. And the absorption peak at 425 cm−1 was caused by the CoN stretching vibration [58]. The above absorption peaks were mainly from the 2-methylimidazole, indicating that the Co ion successfully coordinated with 2-methylimidazole to form ZIF-67. In addition, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed several weak absorption peaks from NCDs compared to ZIF-67. The absorption peak at 3634 cm−1 was due to the OH stretching vibration; the absorption peaks at 1725 and 1670 cm−1 corresponded to the CO stretching vibration; the absorption peak at 1552 cm−1 was caused by the NH bending vibration. The doping of NCDs made 100NCDs/ZIF-67 contain more oxygen-containing functional groups, which might provide more active sites in the reaction, and was conducive to improving the catalytic performance [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 for catalytic degradation of BPA

ce:section.ce:para:
Add 30, 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 into 100 mL of 20 mg/L BPA solution, and add PMS to a concentration of 0.4 mmol/L. The effect of catalyst dosage on the degradation of BPA was investigated under this experimental condition. As the dosage of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 increased from 30 mg/L to 200 mg/L, the degradation efficiency of BPA was gradually improved (Fig. 4a). When the dosage of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was 30 mg/L and 50 mg/L, after reaction for 40 min, the removal of BPA was 91.38 % and 96.74 %, respectively. When the dosage of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 increased to 100, 150 and 200 mg/L, BPA could be completely removed within 40 min, 30 min and 20 min, respectively. In the reaction system, Co2+ on the 100NCDs/ZIF-67 can effectively activate PMS to produce SO4 •− (Eq. (1)), and SO4 •− further reacts with H2O or OH− to produce •OH (Eqs. (2)-(3)). The produced SO4 •− and •OH can effectively degrade BPA. When the dosage of catalyst increased, the sites of activated PMS increased, and more free radicals were produced for the degradation of BPA.

50 mg/L 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was added into 100 mL BPA solution containing 5, 10, 20, 30 and 40 mg/L, respectively, and PMS was added to a concentration of 0.4 mmol/L for the degradation experiment, so as to explore the effect of BPA concentration on BPA degradation. With the increase of BPA concentration, the removal efficiency of BPA decreased (Fig. 4b). When the BPA concentrations were 5 mg/L and 10 mg/L, BPA could be completely degraded within 10 min and 20 min, respectively. When the BPA concentration was increased to 20, 30 and 40 mg/L, after reaction for 40 min, the removal of BPA was 96.74 %, 83.58 % and 58.35 %, respectively. When the dosage of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 and PMS were fixed, the amount of free radicals produced in the system was also almost fixed. With the increase of BPA concentration, the system could not provide enough free radicals for the degradation of excess BPA, and the intermediates generated during the degradation process would also compete free radicals with BPA. Ultimately led to the decrease of BPA removal efficiency.

To explore the effect of PMS dosage on BPA degradation, 50 mg/L 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was added into 100 mL 20 mg/L BPA solution, and PMS was added to the concentrations of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mmol/L respectively. When the PMS concentrations were 0.2 mmol/L and 0.4 mmol/L, reacting for 40 min, the removal of BPA were 70.40 % and 96.74 %, respectively (Fig. 4c). When the PMS concentration increased to 0.6 mmol/L, BPA could be completely degraded within 20 min. However, when the concentration continued to increase to 0.8 mmol/L, the improvement of BPA removal efficiency was no longer obvious, and the efficiency was close to that of 0.6 mmol/L. When the concentration of PMS was 1.0 mmol/L, the removal efficiency of BPA even decreased. When the concentration of PMS in the reaction system was low, the increased PMS could react with the catalyst to produce more free radicals for the degradation of BPA, and the removal efficiency of BPA was significantly improved. When the added PMS reached a certain concentration, SO4 •− and •OH generated in the system would consume excess PMS to generate free radicals with lower oxidation capacity (Eq. S1–Eq. S3). The excess SO4 •− and •OH would also undergo quenching reaction within and between each other (Eq. S4–Eq. S6), resulting in the reduction of available PMS and free radicals, thus the removal efficiency of BPA was no longer significantly improved or even decreased.

To explore the effect of pH on BPA degradation, H2SO4 and NaOH were used to adjust the pH in 100 mL 20 mg/L BPA solution, so that the initial pH of the reaction system was 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11, respectively. Then 50 mg/L 100NCDs/ZIF-67 and 0.4 mmol/L PMS were added. As shown in Fig. 4d, the order of BPA removal efficiency at different pH values was as follows: pH = 5 > pH not adjusted > pH = 7 > pH = 9 > pH = 3 > pH = 11. The reaction system could maintain efficient removal of BPA in the pH range of 5 to 9, and showed the highest removal efficiency at pH 5. While the degradation of BPA was significantly inhibited at pH 3 and 11. This might be because under strongly acidic conditions, excess H+ would react with SO4 •− and •OH (Eq. S7–Eq. S8), resulting in fewer free radicals for the degradation of BPA. However, under strong alkali environment, excessive OH− would eliminate PMS and the generated SO4 •− (Eq. S9–Eq. S10). In addition, PMS has a pKa of 9.4. At pH higher than or equal to 9.4, PMS exists mainly in the form of SO5 2− in solution. Compared with HSO5 −, SO5 2− has a lower oxidation capacity and is less capable of being activated to form SO4 •− and •OH. Meanwhile, under alkaline conditions, the catalytic potential of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS would be reduced by the formation of cobalt hydroxyl complexes (Eq. S11).

Comparing the results of existing studies on BPA degradation by PMS catalyzed by modified MOF materials or carbon dots, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 has the optimal performance of BPA catalytic degradation, and the reaction can realize the efficient removal of BPA in a relatively short time, with mild reaction conditions, low Cuihua Jiu dosage, and high mineralization rate, and the specific comparisons are shown in the Table S2.

To explore the effects of coexisting substances on the degradation of BPA by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS, anions and fulvic acid with concentration gradients were added into the reaction solution respectively to conduct BPA degradation experiments. As shown in Fig. 5a–d, the anions Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and HCO3 − inhibited the removal of BPA, and the degree of inhibition was as follows: HCO3 − > SO4 2− > Cl− ≈ NO3 −. The inhibitory effects of Cl− and NO3 − on BPA degradation were small. When the concentration increased from 5 mmol/L to 20 mmol/L, BPA could be completely degraded within 20 min. While the inhibitory effects of SO4 2− and HCO3 − on BPA removal were stronger. When the concentration of SO4 2− increased to 20 mmol/L, BPA could not be completely degraded within 40 min, and the removal of BPA was 92.19 % at 40 min. When the concentration of HCO3 − was 20 mmol/L, the removal of BPA was only 26.23 % after reaction for 40 min. This might be because the SO4 •− and •OH produced in the system could react with the above anions to form free radicals with lower oxidation capacity (Eq. S12–Eq. S23). In contrast, H2PO4 − showed a weak promoting effect on the removal of BPA (Fig. 5e). When the concentration of H2PO4 − in the system was 5 mmol/L, the removal of BPA was 93.28 % for 10 min, which was 12.88 % higher than that without H2PO4 −. When the H2PO4 − concentration was further increased to 10 mmol/L and 20 mmol/L, the removal of BPA was 94.80 % and 97.04 % at 10 min, respectively. Although H2PO4 − could also react with SO4 •− and •OH to form •H2PO4 with low oxidation capacity (Eq. S24–Eq. S25), meanwhile, H2PO4 − could combine with PMS and pollutants to weaken the dissociation energy of OO asymmetric bonds in PMS, or form a complex with Co2+ to accelerate the removal of BPA [60].

The presence of fulvic acid in the system also inhibited the degradation of BPA (Fig. 5f). When the concentration of fulvic acid in the solution was 5 mg/L, the removal efficiency of BPA was greatly affected in the first 10 min. When the concentration of fulvic acid increased to 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L, the removal of BPA was significantly inhibited, and the effect was enhanced with the increase of concentrations. The removal of BPA was 82.17 % and 32.57 % after reacting for 15 min, respectively. Compared with the system without fulvic acid, the removal of BPA decreased by 16.66 % and 66.26 %. On the one hand, fulvic acid could compete with BPA for the limited active species in the system. On the other hand, fulvic acid might bind with functional groups (phenol and nitrogen-containing sites, etc.) to form chelation with the active center of Co on the surface of 100NCDs/ZIF-67, preventing exposure of the active site, and thus reducing the catalytic efficiency of 100NCDs/ZIF-67. Besides, imidazole rings in 100NCDs/ZIF-67 could interact with aromatic rings in fulvic acid through special π-π stacking interactions, which might also lead to a decrease in the number of active sites for PMS activation. Nevertheless, when the concentration of fulvic acid in the solution was 20 mg/L, BPA could be completely removed within 40 min.

Twenty-five mg of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was added to 250 mL of 10 mg/L BPA solution, while PMS was added into the solution to make its concentration 0.4 mmol/L. 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was used to activate PMS for BPA degradation under this condition for four times to investigate its reuse performance. When used for the first time, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS could completely remove BPA within 8 min (Fig. S6). When used for the second time, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 maintained high catalytic activity, and the complete removal of BPA was achieved within 10 min. The catalytic performance of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed a decrease in the third and fourth uses, the BPA removal was 91.24 % and 80.60 % at 10 min, respectively. The decrease in the catalytic performance of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 for the degradation of BPA might be attributed to the loss of the material collected in recycling and the leaching of the active components during the repeated catalytic degradation process. Nevertheless, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 maintained efficient removal of BPA after four reuse cycles.

The crystal structure changes of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 before and after repeated use were compared, and the results were shown in Fig. S7. The diffraction peaks of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 after the reaction appeared in the same position as before the reaction, and the diffraction peak still showed sharp peak shape, indicating that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 could maintain a good crystal structure during the reuse process. The morphology of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 after repeated use was also observed and compared with that before reaction (Fig. S8). After the reaction, most of the 100NCDs/ZIF-67 particles maintained a good rhombohedral crystal structure, but the surface of the crystal particles was slightly rougher compared with that before the reaction, and some particles had small holes on the surface. Only a small portion of the rhombic dodecahedral morphology of the particles was damaged, which might be caused by the dissolution of cobalt ions in the catalytic degradation reaction, or the cracking of crystal particles caused by agitation in the reaction process. The Co loss after each round of repeated use was quantified by ICP-MS test as shown Fig. S9. The loss of Co was 0.03, 0.08, 0.15 and 0.27 mg/L after rounds 1 to 4, respectively, and a significant correlation between the decrease in catalytic activity and the loss of Co was verified based on the trend finding. Overall, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 could maintain good crystal morphology and structure after reaction, indicating that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 material had good reuse potential.

The catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS for BPA degradation was investigated in real water bodies such as river water and lake water, respectively. The BPA degradation experiments were carried out in the river water of Chongqing section of the Jialing River in China, and in a lake water from Chongqing, China, and the BPA removal was compared with that in the ultrapure water background, and the water quality data taken are shown in Table S3.

The experimental results are shown in Fig. S10. In the ultrapure water reaction environment, the pollutant BPA could be completely removed within 8 min, with a reaction rate of 0.2627 min−1. In contrast, in the river water and lake water, the removal effect of BPA decreased, and after 20 min of reaction, the removal rate of BPA was 84.90 % and 80.36 %, with a reaction rate of 0.0923 min-1, respectively. Rates of 0.0923 min−1 and 0.0787 min−1. In real water, the decrease in BPA removal by catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS could be attributed to the quenching of reactive species in the reaction system by the presence of various anions in the water column or the interaction with free radicals such as SO4 •− and •OH, which reacted with free radicals to generate free radicals with lower oxidizing capacity. In addition, organic matter present in the water column competes with the contaminant BPA for the limited number of free radicals in the reaction system. Overall, however, the catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 is still highly efficient in removing BPA in real-world aqueous environments and shows good potential for real-world aqueous applications.

In addition to excellent catalytic performance, the cost-effectiveness of the 100NCDs/ZIF-67 catalyst also highlights its potential for practical applications. The synthesis process uses low-cost and widely available precursors, and is conducted under mild conditions without high-temperature pyrolysis or toxic solvents. The estimated cost of producing 1 g of catalyst is approximately 0.1 EUR. Based on the effective dosage used in this study (100 mg/L), the estimated treatment cost for 1 ton of BPA-contaminated water is approximately 10 EUR. Furthermore, the catalyst demonstrates high reusability, retaining >80 % removal efficiency after four cycles, which further reduces long-term operational cost to about 3 EUR per ton. These advantages suggest that the catalyst is economically feasible and scalable for real-world wastewater treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 for catalytic degradation of BPA

ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the active species produced by activation of PMS by 100NCDs/ZIF-67, methanol was added as the quencher for SO4 •− and •OH, tert-butanol as the quencher for •OH, p-benzoquinone as the quencher for O2 •−, and L-histidine as the quencher for 1O2, respectively, and BPA degradation experiments were conducted. When 0.1 mol/L and 0.5 mol/L methanol were added, the removal of BPA was significantly inhibited (Fig. 6a), and the removal of BPA was reduced by 51.44 % and 73.88 % compared with the original reaction system. When the methanol dosage increased to 1.0 mol/L, the inhibition of BPA removal was no longer significantly increased, indicating the presence of SO4 •− or •OH in the reaction system. The inhibition of BPA degradation under the action of tert-butanol was lower than that under the action of methanol (Fig. 6b), so it could be inferred that both SO4 •− and •OH existed in the system. When the concentration of p-benzoquinone in the reaction system was increased from 5 mmol/L to 20 mmol/L, the removal of BPA decreased by 56.26 %, 56.84 % and 70.61 % compared with the original control system (Fig. 6c), indicating the presence of O2 •− in the system. When 5 mmol/L L-histidine was added to the reaction system, the degradation of BPA was severely inhibited (Fig. 6d). With the increase of L-histidine addition, the inhibition of BPA degradation also increased. When the concentration of L-histidine was 20 mmol/L, the final removal of BPA was only 2.07 %, indicating that 1O2 existed in the system and it played a major role in the degradation of BPA.

To further identify the active species, the reaction system was characterized by EPR. In Fig. 7a, there was an obvious quadruple peak, with a peak ratio of 1:2:2:1, which was a typical characteristic signal belonging to MPO-•OH. In addition, weak characteristic peaks belonging to DMPO-SO4 •− signals surrounding DMPO-•OH signals could also be observed in Fig. 7a, and the ratio of peaks is 1:1:1:1:1:1, indicating the existence of both •OH and SO4 •−. An obvious signal of DMMPO-O2 •− could be observed from Fig. 7b, indicating that O2 •− was also present in the system. In addition, a triple peak with a peak ratio of about 1: 1: 1, which belongs to the characteristic signal of TEMP-1O2, appeared in Fig. 7c. The last peak was slightly lower than the first two peaks, which might be caused by solution obstruction. Combined with the quenching experiment results, it could be determined that SO4 •−, •OH, O2 •− and 1O2 existed in the 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS system.

Catalytic activation of PMS to degrade pollutants often involves the electron transfer process. In order to explore whether there is an electron transfer pathway in the process of degradation of BPA by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS, chronoamperometry was used to detect the changes in the current of the reaction system that successively added PMS and BPA. For the first 200 s, a stable current response could be observed (Fig. 8 ). However, when PMS was added to the electrolyte (200 s), the current curves of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 and ZIF-67 showed instantaneous fluctuations. The current change at this time was caused by the catalyst activating PMS to produce free radicals. Subsequently, when BPA was added at 400 s, the current showed a significant decrease, indicating electron transfer occurred among the catalyst, PMS and BPA. The current variation amplitude of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was about 2.4 times that of ZIF-67, indicating that the electron transfer efficiency of the reaction system with 100NCDs/ZIF-67 as catalyst was higher than that of ZIF-67, and the doping of NCDs in 100NCDs/ZIF-67 could improve the electron transfer efficiency. In addition, the test results also indicated that there was also a non-radical effect of electron transfer in the degradation of BPA by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS.

To further understand the electron transfer process, linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) curves of 100NCDs/ZIF-67, 100NCDs/ZIF-67/PMS and 100NCDs/ZIF-67/PMS/BPA systems were tested. The current in the 100NCDs/ZIF-67/PMS system was significantly higher than that in the system with only 100NCDs/ZIF-67 catalyst (Fig. S11), which was due to the interaction between 100NCDs/ZIF-67 and PMS to form a metastable reaction complex. After further addition of the pollutant BPA, an increase in current was observed. This increase in current was due to electron transfer between BPA and the metastable reaction complex, which contributed to its own degradation. Therefore, the LSV test once again proved that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS had a non-free radical effect on the degradation of BPA.

To explore the active site of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS, XPS characterization was performed to analyze the changes of surface element chemical states before and after 100NCDs/ZIF-67 reaction. After 100NCDs/ZIF-67 reaction, the characteristic peaks of Co 2p, O 1 s, N 1 s and C1s did not change significantly, only the intensity of the peaks decreased slightly compared with that before the reaction (Fig. 9a). The peak fitting results of Co 2p, O 1 s and N 1 s fine spectra were further analyzed (Fig. 9b–d). From the N 1 s fine spectra, it could be seen that the binding energies of pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen did not change after the reaction of 100NCDs/ZIF-67, but proportion of pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen changed. Pyridine nitrogen decreased from 88 % to 86 %, and pyrrole nitrogen increased from 12 % to 14 %. In the O 1 s fine spectrum, the binding energy of O–C=O at 533.5 eV and CoO at 531.7 eV decreased after the reaction, and the content of O–C=O increased from 15 % to 24 %, and the content of CoO decreased from 85 % to 76 %. In addition, the binding energies of the characteristic peaks and satellite peaks of Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 also shifted to the lower field after 100NCDs/ZIF-67 reaction. These results indicated that pyridine nitrogen, O–C=O and cobalt ions might be the main active sites of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS.

Based on the above experimental results, the possible mechanism of action for the activation of PMS by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was proposed. In 100NCDs/ZIF-67, cobalt ions mainly existed in the form of Co2+, which activated PMS to produce SO4 •− and Co3+ (Eq. S26). Meanwhile, by capturing electrons from PMS, the generated Co3+ was reduced to Co2+, and SO5 •− (Eq. S27) was produced. The redox cycle formed by the exchange between the Co2+ and Co3+ states ensured the continuation of the reaction. Pyridine nitrogen with long pair electrons could promote the transfer of free-flowing π electrons from sp2 carbon of the NCDs material to activate PMS and produce SO4 •− and •OH (Eq. S30–Eq. S32) [61]. In addition, as the active site, −C=O could further promote the activation of PMS by accelerating electron transfer (Eq. S28–Eq. S29) [62]. The SO4 •− and •OH generated in the system could be further converted to O2 •− (Eq. S33–Eq. S35). The produced O2 •− could also react with H2O or •HO2 to generate 1O2 (Eq. S36–Eq. S37). In addition, 1O2 could be converted by SO5 •− and SO5 2− produced during PMS activation (Eq. S38–Eq. S41).

The intermediates generated during the degradation of BPA were analyzed using LC-MS. A total of 15 intermediates were detected, and three possible degradation pathways of BPA in the reaction system were proposed, as shown in Fig. 10 . In pathway I, •OH produced by the system would attack the electronic-rich benzene ring on BPA and form monohydroxylated BPA (P1) through hydroxylation reaction, and P1 would continue to be attacked by addition and electrophilic attack to form dihydroxylated BPA (R1) [63]. Subsequently, P1 and R1 were converted to the quinone products P3 and P2 through the loss of H+, respectively. P2 and P3 opened the ring to form carboxylating intermediates P6 and P4. On this basis, P4 continued to be oxidized to form P5. In Pathway II, the active species could attack the methyl group between the two benzene rings, resulting in the conversion of BPA to hydroxylated BPA, and the other methyl group on the hydroxylated BPA was further replaced by a carboxyl group to form P8 [64]. P8 was continuously oxidized through ring opening to form P9 and P11. The intermediate P11 continued to form monocyclic hydroxylated phenolic derivatives through CC cleavage, which were oxidized to form P7. In Pathway III, •OH and SO4 •− could extract the H of the –OH group on BPA to form the resonance-stable phenoxy BPA radical P10, which involved the migration and dislocation of a single e− on the O atom to the ortho C atom [65]. P10 was further involved in hydroxylation to form the addition product R2, which could also be converted into its isomerized form P3. P3 went on to form P4 and P5 through bond cleavage and carboxylation. Under the continuous action of SO4 •−, •OH, O2 •− and 1O2, the above compounds undergowent a series of pyrolysis, oxidation and ring-opening processes to form small molecular intermediates P12, P13, P14 and P15, which were finally oxidized to CO2 and H2O.

To investigate the degradation of the pollutant BPA in the PMS system activated by catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67, the changes in the concentrations of PMS and total organic carbon in the reaction solution were determined over time, and the trends are shown in Fig. S12. As the catalytic degradation reaction proceeded, the amount of PMS in the solution was first rapidly consumed in the first 20 min, and then the activation rate of PMS slowed down between 40 min. After 40 min, the PMS activation reaction tended to stop and the final activation rate was 80.89 %. The mineralization process of the pollutant BPA showed a similar trend to the activation process of PMS. In the first 20 min of the reaction, the catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 rapidly activated PMS, which led to the rapid degradation of BPA under the action of free radicals and non-free radicals, and the mineralization rate of BPA reached 51.93 % at 20 min. After 20 min, the mineralization rate of BPA gradually slowed down until the reaction was 90 min with a mineralization rate of BPA of 62.01 %, and the catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 activated PMS showed high mineralization effect on pollutant BPA. In addition, although 100 % removal of BPA was achieved, the mineralization rate did not reach 100 %, indicating that degradation intermediates were still present in the reaction system. Therefore, it is necessary to analyze the generated intermediates to speculatively understand the degradation pathway of BPA in this system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of enhanced catalytic performance of 100NCDs/ZIF-67

ce:section.ce:para: N2 adsorption-desorption test was performed on ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 to obtain their specific surface area and pore size distribution. The adsorption curves of both materials showed a rapid increase in nitrogen adsorption amount under low relative pressure (Fig. S13), indicating that a micropore filling process occurred. Which conforms to the characteristics of type I adsorption isotherm [66]. The pore size of ZIF-67 is mainly distributed between 1 and 10 nm, while that of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 is mainly distributed between 1 and 7 nm. The specific surface area and average pore size of the materials were calculated by BET adsorption isothermal model and BJH method, respectively. The single point method and t-plot method were used to calculate the total pore volume and micropore volume, respectively, the calculation results were shown in Table S4. Compared with ZIF-67, the specific surface area of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 only increased slightly, which was 1537.65 and 1590.63 m2/g, respectively. Therefore, it is suggested that the doping of NCDs did not significantly change the specific surface area of the materials, and might have little effect on the adsorption properties. In addition, the total pore volume of both ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was about 0.64 cm3/g. While in 100NCDs/ZIF-67, micropores accounted for about 92.3 % of the total pore volume. In ZIF-67, the micropores account for 89.4 %. Due to the high proportion of micropores in 100NCDs/ZIF-67, the average pore size was smaller than that of ZIF-67.

The higher microporosity percentage of the material 100NCDs/ZIF-67 resulted in it exhibiting a smaller average pore size of 6.52 nm compared to ZIF-67 [67]. As shown by the N2 adsorption-desorption characterization, the modification of ZIF-67 by NCDs had a lesser effect on the specific surface area and the total pore volume of the material, whereas it had a significant effect on the percentage of the micropores in the pores of the material and the average pore size. The material 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed a higher percentage of micropores and a smaller average pore size than ZIF-67. Combined with the SEM image analysis, this may be due to the modification of NCDs, which makes the 100NCDs/ZIF-67 have a more angular and regular crystal morphology and a more orderly arrangement of the crystal particles compared with ZIF-67, which makes the distribution of the pore size inside the material more even, and thus the pore size distribution inside the material is more uniform, and the modification of NCDs has a significant effect on the specific surface area and total pore volume. Thus, the percentage of microporous pore volume increases and the average pore size decreases.

The effect of NCDs doping on the adsorption properties of the composite was further investigated. Although the N2 adsorption-desorption results showed that ZIF-67 and NCDs/ZIF-67 had high specific surface area, the adsorption of BPA was poor for all the materials (Fig. S14). The concentration of BPA decreased significantly only within 3 min by the adsorption of ZIF-67 and NCDs/ZIF-67. Then, with the increase of adsorption time, the adsorption of BPA by all materials was in a dynamic equilibrium state. After 30 min of adsorption, the removal of BPA by ZIF-67, 10NCDs/ZIF-67, 20NCDs/ZIF-67, 50NCDs/ZIF-67, 100NCDs/ZIF-67, 300NCDs/ZIF-67 and 500NCDs/ZIF-67 were 2.44 %, 3.14 %, 3.83 %, 3.14 %, 4.88 %, 3.14 % and 4.18 %, respectively. The doping of NCDs did not significantly improve the adsorption performance of the composites for BPA. Therefore, NCDs doping did not improve the adsorption performance of the material.

Static contact angles of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were measured, and the results were shown in Fig. S15. The contact angle of ZIF-67 was 129° and it exhibited hydrophobicity, which was mainly caused by the hydrophobicity of conjugated imidazole rings [68]. After doping NCDs in 100NCDs/ZIF-67, the contact angle decreased to 118°, indicating that doping NCDs increased the hydrophilicity of materials. The increase in the hydrophilicity of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 is conducive to its dispersion in the aqueous solution, and increases the contact active sites of the catalyst with PMS and BPA, which could improve the catalytic degradation efficiency of the pollutant.

In addition, according to Section 3.4.2, pyridine nitrogen is one of the active sites for activating PMS. Therefore, the XPS fine spectral peak fitting results of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were compared (Fig. S16). In the C 1 s fine spectrum, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed an additional CN characteristic peak at 286.8 eV, possibly due to doping of NCDs. In N 1 s fine spectrum, the proportion of pyridine nitrogen in 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was higher than that in ZIF-67. Pyridine nitrogen was widely known as the active center, which might play an important role in the catalytic reaction [69]. The FTIR results also proved that the doping of NCDs made 100NCDs/ZIF-67 contain more oxygen-containing functional groups. The higher proportion of pyridine nitrogen and more oxygen-containing functional groups in 100NCDs/ZIF-67 might make 100NCDs/ZIF-67 have more catalytic sites than ZIF-67.

In order to investigate the effect of NCDs doping on the surface electron transfer efficiency of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 during the catalytic reaction, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 were measured. The EIS curves of ZIF-67 and 100NCDs/ZIF-67 showed an arc in the high-frequency and mid-frequency regions, respectively, which represented the surface impedance and charge transfer impedance of the catalytic materials (Fig. S17). The low-frequency region showed a slanted straight line that represented the Warburg impedance of ions in the material [70]. 100NCDs/ZIF-67 displayed a clear semi-arc in the high frequency region, while ZIF-67's semi-circle could not be observed, possibly because the impedance of the ZIF-67 surface was too high to form a semi-circle [71]. Besides, compared with ZIF-67, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 had a smaller semi-arc diameter in the mid-frequency region, indicating that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 had a smaller charge transfer impedance. It could be seen that the doping of NCDs made 100NCDs/ZIF-67 have lower surface impedance and charge transfer impedance, which led to the improvement of the electron transfer efficiency during the catalytic reaction.

This result was also consistent with the test result that the change in current intensity of 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was 2.4-fold bigger than that of ZIF-67 in the chronoamperometry method, demonstrating the higher electron transfer efficiency; linear scanning voltammetry (Fig. S8) further confirmed the synergistic activation of the PMS by 100NCDs/ZIF-67 with the non-radical pathway dominating the degradation of BPA; while the Post-reaction XPS characterization identified pyridine-N, O-C=O groups and Co2+ as the main active sites. Specifically, Co2+ activated PMS through redox cycling, while the O-C=O group stabilized the metastable intermediates. Pyridine-N enhanced π-electron precipitation within the carbon matrix and facilitated rapid interfacial electron transfer. Overall, these findings elucidate that the NCD-modified ZIF-67 composites can significantly improve the electron transfer efficiency and the mechanism of action.

The catalyst NCDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized by modifying ZIF-67 with NCDs, which was used to activate PMS to degrade BPA. When the doping amount of NCDs in NCDs/ZIF-67 was 100 μL, the optimal catalytic performance was obtained, and the removal of BPA could reach 94.87 % after 30 min reaction, which was 52.61 % higher than that of ZIF-67. The characterization results showed that 100NCDs/ZIF-67 was synthesized successfully, and the doping of NCDs did not change the original morphology and structure of the material. In comparison, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 crystallized better. The presence of Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 − and fulvic acid inhibited the removal of BPA, while H2PO4 − promoted the removal of BPA. Catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67 had good reuse performance. After repeated use for four times, the removal of BPA could still reach 80.60 % for 10 min, the crystal morphology and structure could be maintained. The quenching experiment and EPR characterization showed that SO4 •−、•OH、O2 •− and 1O2 existed in the reaction system. 1O2 played a major role in the degradation of BPA. The results of i-t and LSV tests indicated that non-radical effect of electron transfer was also present in the system. According to the results of LC-MS, three possible degradation pathways of BPA were proposed. The doping of NCDs did not significantly improve the adsorption properties of the composites, but could improve the hydrophilicity of the composites, increase the catalytic active sites of the composites, and reduce the surface impedance and charge transfer impedance of the materials during the catalytic reaction, thus improving the degradation performance of BPA.

The modification of NCDs improved the catalytic performance and hydrophilicity of the catalyst 100NCDs/ZIF-67, 100NCDs/ZIF-67 still suffered from catalyst loss in the reuse experiments. Therefore, further magnetic modification of the catalytic materials or preparation of catalytic membranes can be considered to enhance the recovery of the materials. Meanwhile, the effects of additional modifications on the catalytic properties of the materials need to be considered.

Jing Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xinlei Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Chunyan Hu: Writing – review & editing. Wentao Huang: Investigation. Junxing Gao: Supervision. Jianjun Liang: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
